questionText: Aman's expense is 30% more than Vimal's and Vimal's expense is 10% less than Raman's. If the sum of their expenses is ₹6447, then what would be Aman's expense?

steps.explanation: Let Raman's expense be ₹x.
Aman's expense is 30% more than Vimal's.
Sum of all three expenses equals ₹6447.
Calculate Aman's expense.

solution.shortcut: Let Raman = 100, Vimal = 90, Aman = 117. Ratio sum = 307. Aman's share = (117/307) × 6447 = 2457.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'more than' with 'of' — 30% more means multiplying by 1.30, not 0.30.

questionText: Two tailors X and Y are paid a total of ₹550 per week by their employer. If X is paid 20 percent more than the sum paid to Y, how much is Y paid per week?

steps.explanation: Let Y's weekly pay be ₹x.
Total pay equation.
Y's weekly pay is ₹250.

solution.commonMistake: Setting X + Y = 550 but computing X as 20% of total instead of 20% more than Y.

questionText: Prithvi spent ₹89745 on his college fees, ₹51291 on Personality Development Classes and the remaining 27% of the total amount he had as cash with him. What was the total amount?

steps.explanation: Let total amount be ₹x. The spent portion is (100 − 27)% = 73% of x.
Solve for x.

solution.commonMistake: Using 27% as the spent fraction instead of the remaining fraction.

questionText: If a number is reduced by 40% it becomes two-thirds of another number. What is the ratio of the first number to the second number?

steps.explanation: Let the two numbers be x and y. 'Reduced by 40%' means 60% of x.
Cross multiply to find the ratio.

solution.commonMistake: Treating 'reduced by 40%' as 40% of x instead of 60% of x.

questionText: To meet a government requirement, a bottler must test 5 percent of its spring water and 10 percent of its sparkling water for purity. If a customer ordered 120 cases of spring water and 80 cases of sparkling water, then what percent of all the cases must the bottler test before he can send it out?

steps.explanation: Cases to be tested from spring water.
Cases to be tested from sparkling water.
Total cases tested as percentage of total cases.

solution.commonMistake: Simply averaging 5% and 10% to get 7.5% without weighting by number of cases.

questionText: When income tax is 3 paise in a rupee, a person's net income is ₹237650. What will it be when the income tax is raised to 7 paise?

steps.explanation: 3 paise in a rupee means 3% income tax. Net income = (100 − 3)% of gross.
When tax is 7 paise (7%), net income = (100 − 7)% of gross.

solution.shortcut: Find gross from 97% net, then apply 93%.
solution.commonMistake: Subtracting 4% (difference in tax rates) directly from net income instead of recalculating from gross.

questionText: A monthly return railway ticket costs 25 percent more than a single ticket. A week's extension can be had for the former by paying 5 percent of the monthly ticket's cost. If the money paid for the monthly ticket (with extension) is ₹84, the price of the single ticket is

steps.explanation: Let single ticket cost = ₹x. Monthly ticket = 125% of x.
Extension cost = 5% of monthly ticket.
Total cost with extension = ₹84.

solution.shortcut: Monthly + extension = 105% of monthly = 105% × 125% of single = 131.25% of single = 84, so single = 64.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to add the extension cost (5%) to the monthly ticket price.

questionText: In limestone, 40% is calcium and the rest is carbon and oxygen. If in 20 kg of limestone, there is 9.4 kg of oxygen, then what is the percentage of carbon in it?

steps.explanation: Percentage of oxygen in limestone.
Percentage of carbon = 100% − calcium% − oxygen%.

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that 'rest' means everything except calcium, not just carbon.

questionText: In a class of 72 children, children are seated in rows and columns in such a way that the number of children in each row is 12.5% more than the number of children in each column. How many children are there in each row?

steps.explanation: Let number per column = c. Number per row = 112.5% of c = (9/8)c.
Total children = rows × children per row = columns × children per column. Since rows = c and columns = r (or rows × per_row = 72).
Children per row = 9.

solution.shortcut: If per_column = c, per_row = 9c/8. c × (9c/8) = 72 → c = 8, row = 9.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing rows and columns in the multiplication.

questionText: The owner of a boutique decides to calculate the percentage of customers who purchase hats. If 40 percent of the store's customers decide to purchase items, and of those customers 15 percent purchase hats, what percent of the store's customers purchase hats?

steps.explanation: Hat buyers = 15% of (40% of total customers).

solution.commonMistake: Adding 15 and 40 instead of multiplying.

questionText: In a market research project, 20% opted for Nirma detergent whereas 60% opted for Surf Blue detergent. The remaining individuals were not certain. If the difference between those who opted for Surf Blue and those who were uncertain was 720, how many respondents were covered in the survey?

steps.explanation: Difference between Surf Blue (60%) and uncertain (20%) = 40% of total = 720.

solution.commonMistake: Using 60% − 20% of Nirma instead of the uncertain group percentage.

questionText: In an examination it is required to get 36% of the aggregate marks to pass. A student gets 198 marks and is declared failed by 36 marks. What is the maximum aggregate marks a student can get?

steps.explanation: Pass marks = 198 + 36 = 234.
Pass marks = 36% of total. Find total.

solution.commonMistake: Using 198 as pass marks instead of 198 + 36.

questionText: In a test, minimum passing percentage for girls and boys is 35% and 40% respectively. A boy scored 483 marks and failed by 117 marks. What is the minimum passing marks for girls?

steps.explanation: Boy's pass marks = 483 + 117 = 600. Pass marks for boys = 40% of total.
Minimum passing marks for girls = 35% of 1500.
525 does not match any option, so the answer is 'None of these'.

solution.commonMistake: Using boys' pass percentage for girls or not finding total marks first.

modelName: Different pass percentage for different groups

questionText: In an examination it is required to get 40% of the aggregate marks to pass. A student gets 261 marks and is declared failed by 4% marks. What are the maximum aggregate marks a student can get?

steps.explanation: Failed by 4% means the student got (40 − 4)% = 36% of total marks.
725 does not match any given option, so the answer is 'None of these'.

solution.commonMistake: Adding 4% to 40% instead of subtracting: student failed by 4%, so scored only 36%.

questionText: A candidate has to obtain minimum 33% of the total marks to pass. He got 25% of the total marks and failed by 40 marks. The maximum marks are

steps.explanation: Difference between pass % and obtained % = 33 − 25 = 8% of total = 40 marks.

solution.commonMistake: Using 25% or 33% alone instead of their difference.

questionText: In an examination it is required to get 296 of the total maximum aggregate marks to pass. A student gets 259 marks and is decided failed. The difference of marks obtained by the student and that required to pass is 5%. What are the maximum aggregate marks a student can get?

steps.explanation: Difference in marks = 296 − 259 = 37.
This difference is 5% of maximum marks.

solution.commonMistake: Treating 296 as a percentage (it is an absolute number).

modelName: Marks difference as percentage of total

questionText: Two candidates fought an election. One of them got 62% of the total votes and won by 432 votes. What is the total number of votes polled?

steps.explanation: Winner got 62%, loser got 38%. Margin = (62 − 38)% = 24% of total = 432.

solution.commonMistake: Using 62% × total = 432 instead of the margin percentage.

questionText: In a college election between two candidates, one candidate got 55% of the total valid votes. 15% of the votes were invalid. If the total votes were 15200, what is the number of valid votes the other candidate got?

steps.explanation: Other candidate got (100 − 55)% = 45% of valid votes.

solution.commonMistake: Applying 45% to total votes instead of valid votes.

questionText: At an election involving two candidates, 68 votes were declared invalid. The winning candidate secures 52% and wins by 98 votes. The total number of votes polled is

steps.explanation: Let valid votes be x. Winner got 52%, loser got 48%. Margin = 4% of x = 98.
Total polled = valid + invalid = 2450 + 68.

solution.commonMistake: Treating total polled as valid votes without adding invalid votes.

questionText: In a certain assembly constituency election, 80% of voters exercised their voting right and the winning candidate got elected with 65% of votes polled. What percent of total votes did he poll?

steps.explanation: Winner's votes = 65% of votes polled = 65% of 80% of total.

solution.commonMistake: Adding instead of multiplying percentages.

questionText: In an election, a total of 5,00,000 voters participated. A candidate got 2,55,000 votes which was 60% of the total valid votes. What was the percentage of invalid votes?

steps.explanation: Total valid votes: 60% of valid = 255000.
Percentage of invalid votes.

solution.commonMistake: Dividing invalid votes by valid votes instead of total voters.

questionText: 10% of the voters did not cast their vote in an election between two candidates. 10% of the votes polled were found invalid. The successful candidate got 54% of the valid votes and won by a majority of 1620 votes. The number of voters enrolled on the voters' list was

steps.explanation: Let total enrolled voters = x. Votes polled = 90% of x.
Valid votes = 90% of polled = 90% of 90% of x.
Winner got 54%, loser got 46% of valid votes. Margin = 8% of valid = 1620.

solution.commonMistake: Not chaining all three percentage reductions correctly.

questionText: 8% of the people eligible to vote are between 18 and 21 years of age. In an election, 85% of those eligible to vote, who were between 18 and 21, actually voted. In that election, the number of persons between 18 and 21, who actually voted, was what percent of those eligible to vote?

steps.explanation: 18-to-21 group who voted = 85% of 8% of eligible voters.

solution.commonMistake: Adding instead of multiplying the two percentages.

questionText: In an election, 30% of the voters voted for candidate A whereas 60% of the remaining voted for candidate B. The remaining voters did not vote. If the difference between those who voted for candidate A and those who did not vote was 1200, how many individuals were eligible for casting vote in that election?

steps.explanation: Let eligible voters = x. Voted for A = 30% of x.
Remaining after A = 70% of x. B got 60% of that = 42% of x.
Did not vote = 100 − 30 − 42 = 28% of x.
Difference: A − non-voters = (30 − 28)% × x = 2% × x = 1200.

solution.shortcut: Non-voters = 28%, A voters = 30%. Difference = 2% of total = 1200 → total = 60000.
solution.commonMistake: Not calculating who did not vote as a residual percentage.

questionText: While purchasing one item costing ₹400, I had to pay the sales tax at 7% and on another costing ₹6400, the sales tax was 9%. What percent of the sales tax I had to pay, taking the two items together on an average?

steps.explanation: Tax on first item = 7% of ₹400.
Tax on second item = 9% of ₹6400.
Average tax % = total tax / total cost × 100.

solution.commonMistake: Simple average of 7 and 9 giving 8% — ignores different cost weights.

questionText: A student secures 90%, 60% and 54% marks in test papers with 100, 150 and 200 respectively as maximum marks. The percentage of his aggregate is

steps.explanation: Marks scored in each paper.
Total maximum marks = 100 + 150 + 200 = 450.

solution.commonMistake: Averaging the three percentages directly: (90+60+54)/3 = 68% (wrong).

questionText: 1100 boys and 700 girls are examined in a test; 42% of the boys and 30% of the girls pass. The percentage of the total who failed is

steps.explanation: Boys who passed = 42% of 1100 = 462. Girls who passed = 30% of 700 = 210.
Total students = 1100 + 700 = 1800.
Failed = 1800 − 672 = 1128. Percentage failed.

solution.commonMistake: Simply averaging 58% and 70% failure rates without weighting by group size.

questionText: In a certain school, 20% of students are below 8 years of age. The number of students above 8 years of age is $\frac{2}{3}$ of the number of students of 8 years age which is 48. What is the total number of students in the school?

steps.explanation: Students aged exactly 8 = 48. Students above 8 = (2/3) × 48 = 32.
Students at or above 8 = 48 + 32 = 80 = 80% of total (since 20% are below 8).
100 does not match any option, so the answer is 'None of these'.

solution.commonMistake: Using only 'above 8' without including students exactly aged 8.

questionText: In an examination, 5% of the applicants were found ineligible and 85% of the eligible candidates belonged to the general category. If 4275 eligible candidates belonged to other categories, then how many candidates applied for the examination?

steps.explanation: Eligible = 95% of total applicants. Other category eligible = 15% of eligible.

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that 'other categories' = (100 − 85)% = 15% of eligible, not of total.

questionText: Two students appeared at an examination. One of them secured 9 marks more than the other and his marks was 56% of the sum of their marks. The marks obtained by them are:

steps.explanation: Let higher score = a, lower = b. Then a − b = 9 and a = 56% of (a + b).
Let a = 14k, b = 11k. Then 14k − 11k = 9 → k = 3.

solution.shortcut: Ratio a:b = 14:11; difference = 3 parts = 9, k = 3. a = 42, b = 33.
solution.commonMistake: Setting up equation as a = 56% of b instead of 56% of sum.

questionText: At a special sale, 5 tickets can be purchased for the price of 3 tickets. If 5 tickets are purchased at the sale, the amount saved will be what percent of the original price of the 5 tickets?

steps.explanation: Let original price per ticket = ₹100. Original price of 5 = ₹500.
Sale price of 5 = price of 3 = ₹300. Amount saved = 500 − 300 = ₹200.
% saved of original price of 5 tickets.

solution.shortcut: Pay price of 3, save price of 2. Saved % = 2/5 × 100 = 40%.
solution.commonMistake: Computing savings as % of sale price (2/3 × 100 ≈ 66.7%) instead of original price.

questionText: In September 2009, the sales of a product were $\frac{2}{3}$ of that in July 2009. In November 2009, the sales of the product were higher by 5% as compared to September 2009. How much is the percentage of increase in sales in November 2009 with respect to the base figure in July 2009?

steps.explanation: Let July sales = x. September sales = (2/3)x.
November sales = 105% of September = 1.05 × (2/3)x.
Change relative to July = (0.70x − x)/x × 100%.

solution.shortcut: Nov = 105% × 2/3 of July = 70% of July → 30% less than July.
solution.commonMistake: Interpreting 5% increase as increase over July rather than over September.

questionText: If earth's rotational motion increases by 12%, then the relation between a day and hours will be

steps.explanation: Speed and time are inversely proportional. If speed increases by 12%, new speed = 112% of old.

solution.commonMistake: Multiplying 24 by 112% instead of dividing.

questionText: A and B are two fixed points 5 cm apart and C is a point on AB such that AC is 3 cm. If the length of AC is increased by 6%, the length of CB is decreased by

steps.explanation: Original: AB = 5 cm, AC = 3 cm, CB = 2 cm.
Decrease in CB = 2 − 1.82 = 0.18 cm. % decrease.

solution.shortcut: AC increase = 6% × 3 = 0.18 cm. This is a decrease in CB. % decrease of CB = 0.18/2 × 100 = 9%.
solution.commonMistake: Using 6% as the decrease in CB directly, ignoring different base lengths.

questionText: A table shows flight departure data: Up to 15 min delayed = 43%, 15–30 min delayed = 17%, 30–60 min delayed = 12%, more than 60 min delayed = 3%, and on-time = x%. If 1200 flights were delayed, how many flights departed on time?

steps.explanation: Total delayed % = 43 + 17 + 12 + 3 = 75%.
75% of total = 1200 flights → total = 1600.

solution.commonMistake: Using 1200 as total flights instead of as delayed flights.

questionText: A shopkeeper has a certain number of eggs of which 5% are found to be broken. He sells 93% of the remainder and still has 266 eggs left. How many eggs did he originally have?

steps.explanation: Intact eggs = 95% of total x.
Eggs sold = 93% of intact. Remaining = 7% of intact = 266.

solution.commonMistake: Applying 7% directly to x instead of 7% of (95% of x).

questionText: Ganpat went to fruit market with a certain amount of money. With this money he can buy either 50 oranges or 40 mangoes. He retains 10% of the money for taxi fare. If he buys 20 mangoes, the number of oranges he can buy is

steps.explanation: Let total money = ₹x. Price per orange = x/50; price per mango = x/40.
Money for purchases = 90% of x = 9x/10. Cost of 20 mangoes = 20 × (x/40) = x/2.
Remaining after 20 mangoes = 9x/10 − x/2 = 9x/10 − 5x/10 = 4x/10 = 2x/5.
Number of oranges = (2x/5) ÷ (x/50) = (2x/5) × (50/x) = 20.

solution.shortcut: Remaining money = 90% − 50% (20 mangoes) = 40% of total. Oranges = 40% × 50 = 20.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting the 10% taxi fare deduction before spending on fruits.

questionText: A 14.4 kg gas cylinder runs for 104 hours when the smaller burner on the gas stove is fully opened while it runs for 80 hours when the larger burner on the gas stove is fully opened. Which of these values is the closest to the percentage difference in the usage of gas per hour, of the smaller burner over the larger burner?

steps.explanation: Gas per hour for smaller burner = 14.4/104 = 9/65 kg/hr.
Gas per hour for larger burner = 14.4/80 = 9/50 kg/hr.
% difference of smaller over larger = (larger − smaller)/larger × 100.

solution.commonMistake: Comparing smaller over larger vs larger over smaller — direction matters.

questionText: A survey of magazine reading habits is summarised in a table. Column I = % of readers who read only one magazine a week; Column II = number of readers who read two or more magazines a week. Data: P: 75%, 6000; Q: 80%, 3500; R: 60%, 3000; S: 55%, 2700; T: 25%, 4200. The city with the lowest number of magazine-readers is

steps.explanation: For each city: total readers = (2+ readers) / (1 − %one). E.g., City T: (1−0.25)×total = 4200 → total = 4200/0.75 = 5600.

solution.shortcut: T total = 4200/(1−0.25) = 5600 — lowest among all cities.
solution.commonMistake: Not computing total readers; just comparing Col II values.

questionText: Using the magazine survey table (same as Q174), which city has the highest number of magazine-readers who read only one magazine a week?

solution.commonMistake: Using column I percentage directly without computing absolute numbers.

questionText: Using the magazine survey table (same as Q174), the highest number of magazine-readers in any given city is

steps.explanation: From the calculated totals: P=24000, Q=17500, R=7500, S=6000, T=5600.

solution.shortcut: City P has 24000 total readers — the highest.
solution.commonMistake: Selecting 18000 (one-mag readers in P) instead of 24000 (total readers in P).

questionText: Using the magazine survey table (same as Q174), how many magazine-readers in city Q read only one magazine a week?

steps.explanation: City Q: 80% read one mag. Two+ = 3500 = 20% of total. Total = 3500/0.20 = 17500.
One-mag readers = 17500 − 3500 = 14000.

solution.commonMistake: Computing 80% of 3500 instead of finding total first.

questionText: Using the magazine survey table (same as Q174), the total number of all the magazine-readers in the five cities who read only one magazine a week is

steps.explanation: One-mag readers per city: P=18000, Q=14000, R=4500, S=3300, T=1400.

solution.shortcut: Sum of one-mag readers across all cities = 41200.
solution.commonMistake: Adding total readers instead of only one-mag readers.

questionText: If X is 90% of Y, then what percent of X is Y?

steps.explanation: Y as % of X.

solution.commonMistake: Answering 90% (reversing the relationship).

formulas.title: Reverse %
formulas.expression: \text{If } X = p\% \text{ of } Y, \text{ then } Y = \frac{100}{p}\% \text{ of } X

questionText: $x\%$ of $y$ is $y\%$ of

steps.explanation: $x\%$ of $y = \frac{xy}{100}$. Also $y\%$ of $z = \frac{yz}{100}$. Set them equal.

solution.shortcut: x% of y = y% of x always (symmetry of multiplication).
solution.commonMistake: Over-complicating: x% of y = xy/100 = y% of x.

formulas.title: Symmetry
formulas.expression: x\% \text{ of } y = y\% \text{ of } x
